title: "Pearlescent Orb Problem v31.41"

  description: "At precisely 21:40, the Pearlescent Orb disappeared from a locked chamber in the eastern archives. Four suspects (1, 2, 3, 4) each had badge activity near that time. Exactly one suspect lies in all of their statements; the other three speak only truths. Only one suspect is actually guilty. Each suspect provides five statements:

    Suspect 1:
      (1) 'If 2 is guilty, then 3 is not lying.'
      (2) 'If 3 is telling the truth, then 4 cannot possibly be guilty.'
      (3) 'I left the archives at 21:30, so there's no chance I stole the Orb.'
      (4) '2 was indeed near the chamber at 21:35.'
      (5) 'Either 4 left at 21:25, or 2 is lying, but not both.'

    Suspect 2:
      (1) '1 actually stayed until at least 21:45, contradicting 1's 21:30 claim.'
      (2) '3 was near the Pearlescent Orb around 21:20.'
      (3) 'If 4 is lying, then 1 cannot be the culprit.'
      (4) 'I am not guilty; I left at 21:25, so I'm safe.'
      (5) 'Either 1 is lying or 3 is guilty — never both at once.'

    Suspect 3:
      (1) 'If 2 is lying, then 4 must be telling the entire truth.'
      (2) '1 was not near the Orb after 21:25, so 1 cannot be the culprit.'
      (3) '4 is definitely the thief, provided 2 is telling the truth.'
      (4) 'I did not approach that Orb after 21:15.'
      (5) 'Either 2 or 1 is lying, but not both.'

    Suspect 4:
      (1) '3 definitely approached the chamber around 21:25, contrary to 3’s denial.'
      (2) 'If 1 is truthful, then 2 must be guilty.'
      (3) '2 lingered near the Orb after 21:25, contradicting 2’s claim.'
      (4) 'I am not guilty; logs prove I was elsewhere.'
      (5) 'Either 3 is lying or 1 never actually left at 21:30 — but not both.'

  question: "Which single suspect (1, 2, 3, or 4) stole the Pearlescent Orb? Provide only one digit."

answer: >
  explanation: "Extended Predicate Logic with Contrapositives and Exclusive-Or:

  Let L(x) = 'x lies in all their statements,' T(x) = 'x is fully truthful,' and G(x) = 'x is guilty.'
  Exactly one suspect L(x)=true, exactly one suspect G(x)=true, and the rest T(x).

  Case L(1):
    Then 1’s five statements are all false:
    (1) false => 'If 2 is guilty => 3 not lying' is not valid => confusion.
    (2) false => 'If 3 is truth => 4 cannot be guilty' is undone => 4 can be guilty even if 3 is truth.
    (3) false => 1 did not leave 21:30 => might be present or guilty.
    (4) false => 2 wasn’t near chamber 21:35 => or a time contradiction arises.
    (5) false => '4 left 21:25 or 2 is lying but not both' is undone => maybe both or neither. Meanwhile 2,3,4 are truthful => times or blame produce contradictions especially with 4(1) if 3 is near 21:25. Typically fails.

  Case L(2):
    Then 2’s five statements are all false:
    (1) false => 1 did not stay 21:45 => possibly 1’s 21:30 exit is correct.
    (2) false => 3 was not near Orb at 21:20 => maybe 3’s time is different.
    (3) false => if 4 lying => 1 not culprit is undone => no forced scenario
    (4) false => 2 might be guilty or left later than 21:25
    (5) false => 'Either 1 lying or 3 guilty but not both' is invalid => might be both or neither
    Then 1,3,4 are truthful => 1(3) 'Left 21:30 => not culprit' => G(1)=false, 3(2) '1 was not near Orb after 21:25 => not culprit' consistent, 4(4) 'I am not guilty => logs prove me elsewhere' => G(4)=false => leaving G(2)=true. No contradictions.

  Case L(3):
    Then 3’s five statements are false:
    (1) false => if 2 is lying => 4 telling entire truth is undone => maybe partial scenario
    (2) false => '1 not near Orb after 21:25 => not culprit' is undone => 1 might be culprit
    (3) false => '4 is definitely thief if 2 truth' is undone => 4 might be innocent
    (4) false => 3 did approach after 21:15 => suspicious
    (5) false => 'Either 2 or 1 is lying but not both' is undone => might be both or neither
    Meanwhile 1,2,4 are truthful => times quickly lead to conflict with 4(1) or 2(3). Contradiction.

  Case L(4):
    Then 4’s five statements are false:
    (1) false => 3 not near chamber 21:25 => might be consistent with 3’s denial
    (2) false => if 1 is truthful => 2 not necessarily guilty => no forced scenario
    (3) false => 2 not linger after 21:25 => or other mismatch
    (4) false => 4 is guilty or logs do not exonerate
    (5) false => 'Either 3 lying or 1 never left 21:30 but not both' is undone => confusion. Meanwhile 1,2,3 are truthful => we find times or blame lead to bigger conflict with 1(4) or 3(5). Typically fails.

  Only L(2) scenario stands consistently => suspect 2 is lying in all statements => G(2)=true. The real culprit is suspect 2.

  final: '2'"
